title: Quiz: Molecular Spectroscopy
description: Test your understanding of molecular spectroscopy and quantum transitions

questions.question: Which type of spectroscopy uses microwave radiation?
questions.explanation: Rotational transitions have the smallest energy gaps (typically 0.001-0.01 eV), corresponding to microwave radiation. Vibrational uses IR, electronic uses UV-Vis, and core electrons require X-rays.

questions.question: The vibrational frequency of a bond depends on:

options.text: Both force constant and reduced mass

questions.explanation: Vibrational frequency ν = (1/2π)√(k/μ). It depends on both the force constant k (bond strength) and reduced mass μ. Heavier atoms vibrate more slowly; stronger bonds vibrate faster.

questions.question: Why does the O-H stretch appear at higher frequency than the C-O stretch?

options.text: O-H has a larger force constant
O-H has a smaller reduced mass
Both larger force constant and smaller reduced mass

questions.explanation: The O-H stretch (~3500 cm⁻¹) is higher than C-O (~1100 cm⁻¹) because: (1) O-H has a strong force constant, and (2) H is very light, giving a small reduced mass. Both factors increase frequency.

questions.question: For a vibration to be IR active, what must change during the vibration?
questions.explanation: IR activity requires a change in dipole moment during vibration. This allows the oscillating electric field of IR light to couple with the molecular vibration. Raman activity requires polarizability change.

questions.question: The symmetric stretch of CO₂ is IR active because CO₂ is a polar molecule.
questions.explanation: False on two counts! (1) CO₂ is nonpolar (linear, symmetric). (2) The symmetric stretch of CO₂ is NOT IR active because the dipole moment remains zero throughout the vibration. It is Raman active.

questions.question: What is the selection rule for rotational spectroscopy?
questions.explanation: For pure rotational transitions, the selection rule is ΔJ = ±1. Additionally, the molecule must have a permanent dipole moment for microwave absorption.

questions.question: A molecule absorbs light at 500 nm (green). What color does it appear?
questions.explanation: A molecule that absorbs green light (500 nm) appears as the complementary color, which is red. We see the colors that are NOT absorbed (transmitted or reflected).

questions.question: Which molecules can show a pure rotational spectrum? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Only molecules with a permanent dipole moment can absorb microwave radiation for rotational transitions. HCl and H₂O have permanent dipoles; N₂ and CO₂ do not (they're symmetric).
